title: Salting and curing pork from a home pig
subtitle: Dry-salt curing for bacon and pork joints on a small scale.
excerpt: Dry-salt curing preserves pork without specialist equipment: clean containers, salt, and a cool space. This covers the cure mix, the salt-to-meat ratio, the curing time by cut, and the stages from raw joint to finished dry-cured bacon or salt pork.
sourceNotes: Charcuterie: The Craft of Salting, Smoking and Curing, Michael Ruhlman and Brian Polcyn (Norton, 2013). Food Standards Agency, cured meat guidance. River Cottage Handbook No. 8: Curing and Smoking, Steven Lamb (Bloomsbury, 2014).

glossaryTerms.definition: A curing method where the meat is rubbed or packed in salt rather than submerged in brine. The salt draws moisture from the meat by osmosis, creating a concentrated brine around the joint during the cure period.

A blend of table salt and sodium nitrite (typically 0.6% nitrite by weight) used in cured meats to inhibit Clostridium botulinum and provide the characteristic pink colour. Used in small, precisely measured quantities only; do not vary the amount.

The slightly tacky dried surface that forms on cured meat after rinsing and resting in moving air. A good pellicle indicates the surface moisture has been drawn out correctly and improves smoke adhesion if the meat is to be smoked.

content.text: Dry-salt curing is the traditional method for preserving pork from a home pig. It works with belly, loin, and shoulder, and produces bacon or salt pork that keeps for weeks in a cold larder or months in a refrigerator. The key variables are salt concentration and cure time; both depend on the thickness of the joint.
: combine 1 kg non-iodised salt, 500 g sugar, and 5 g
. This is sufficient for approximately 5 kg of pork. Do not vary the curing salt quantity: too little does not provide safe preservation; too much is unsafe for consumption.

Pat the meat dry. Rub the cure mix thoroughly into all surfaces. Pack the joint into a clean non-reactive container (food-grade plastic or stainless steel), skin side down. Refrigerate at 3 to 5 degrees C. After the first 24 hours, liquid will have drawn out of the meat; turn the joint and redistribute this liquid over all surfaces. Repeat daily.

Cure time depends on thickness: 5 to 7 days for a 3 to 4 cm thick belly; 10 to 14 days for a whole loin; 18 to 21 days for a large shoulder joint. The cure is complete when the meat feels firm throughout when pressed; soft patches indicate the cure has not fully penetrated.
Rinse under cold water and pat dry. Rest on a rack in a cool, airy space for 24 hours to allow a
to form. The finished joint can be sliced and refrigerated for use within three weeks, or vacuum-packed and frozen for up to six months.
